item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gramatical error:

Jasper’s Hill” with an arrow pointing north dot the dirt

Unsure what you meant by this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember the original of this and recall being a bit ambivalent about it. I like this version better.

Also, the final picture creeped me out more than a bit, because that distinguished (if shadowy) gentleman bears a striking resemblance to myself, including hair color and texture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpicks
– hide block

These are observations that didn't affect my vote.

  • There are a lot of spots where the clinical tone is not appropriate and florid. E.g. “result in being unable to find SCP at all.” “crisscrossing", “such that an off-road vehicle might use them for recreational purposes.”, "…amidst a world covered in dark forest. The sky above them, still black and darted with dim stars, appears to shimmer somewhat.”, ”the camera person…”, "where an impossibly large hill is visible…”.
  • There are some scattered spots in the writing that display redundancies. E.g. the last two sentences in the description, “Go ahead and head back….”, “…in the brief instance it is visible on D’s camera, the eyes are visible…”, the first and last notes of the second exploration log (“extract/ion” and “within”, respectively), "The woman holding the camera turns the camera…".
  • The author is my favorite when it comes to the creation of MTF names that fit a given situation but I feel like the task force needs to display some specialization that justifies their organization in-universe. I don’t see that with this team, and it sticks out when they are unprepared for the trek.
  • Why wouldn't the initial MTF not try to investigate the cell phone when they find it? It could easily have accessible and relevant information to not only their situation, but the situation of the people they are trying to find and rescue. It’s uncharacteristic of an assumedly intelligent task force. We should at least be given a line explaining that the phone is locked with a passcode or something and will require later decryption of some kind. We know it doesn't have a passcode lock because later in the article, we find that SCP-3785-1 has used it.
  • What are the images doing in the second exploration log besides adding slightly to the ambience? Am I missing a photoshopped subtlety? The Foundation could include such photos of any or all exploration log scenes, but what is the significance of the ones included here? I would argue that if it is solely for ambiance, that it isn’t a bad inclusion, but it isn’t a good one either; the ambience could just as effectively, and maybe more effectively be made by the reader’s imagination alone. They don't contribute much else. Edit: I found out the author took these pictures. I appreciate the effort, and like this tidbit out-of-universe, but it doesn't fix the in-universe part for me.
  • The ATL-9 team is described as being an extraction team (presumably specialized for extractions), but the purpose of D-15’s mission was “to ascertain the location of and recover eleven missing individuals,” which is also just straight up extraction. I guess in a sentence; why don’t we just have extraction teams for extraction teams all the way down?
  • I loved the twist (ha ha) in the second exploration log, but on second look at the article, the reason why it was so effective is because the description is stingy with what should be some pretty important information to include in-universe. It's an instance of Being as Unhelpful as Possible, which is okay imo if there is justification included. (The only justification I see here is to not include it so the story has a more effective twist. Meh.)
  • In Addendum 4, all descriptions of recovered videos are introduced with “Transcript”, except 3785.AV.14 which says “Description”.
  • I read the page source before the actual article, so I knew well enough that the last picture is of Jasper by the filename. When I actually went back to look at the picture in the article, it made it less scary, not more. The image choice is questionable for me; the person pictured not only looks verifiably human (not “humanoid”), but looks kinda depressed, probably because of the lighting and its angle (no offense to your brother!). As an in-universe element, it spikes my empathy rather than my terror. I also find it kind of funny that the guy took a selfie. Nothing wrong with that inherently.
  • I don’t get the inclusion of the feline elements here. Especially after the octopoid eyes.
Positives
– hide block
  • I think the description of SCP is really well done. I love the sentences together that very skillfully paint a picture we are all familiar with (or at least anyone who has traversed some nature trails or looked out a car window); that clearing of power lines. This is a beautiful and relatable image to start the article.
  • There are some really really good sizzle points in this article:
1. "D-15 Teter: That noise (heavy breathing) the power lines (heavy breathing) they’re on the power lines (heavy breathing) the kids we were (pauses) looking for, they’re hung upside down above (heavy breathing) it’s right behind us.”
This was a genuine “o shiiiiit” moment for me as a reader and is where the piece did a 180 and became really good.

2. "The agent coughs and reaches out towards J, but begins to fall upwards, away from the lines.” Another really powerful “OO SHIIIIIIIT” moment for me. (We'll talk about this more later.)

3. “Above the two men on the power line, the dim stars in the sky begin to change. They grow slightly brighter and twist in on themselves revealing many thousands (if not hundreds of thousands) of large, vaguely octopoid eyes that stretch from horizon to horizon.”
Just wow.

4. "The remaining viscera continues to ascend into the sky.” Oh… my god.

5. "7 calls to 911, 10 calls to contact listed as “Momma”” Ooof. Right in the gut.
Criticisms
– hide block
  • It seems as though there were multiple authorial voices that aren’t well blended. We have some negatively-interfering tones; just as the first exploration log is getting somewhat creepy, we are pulled out of that instantly by the inclusion of a person making cat noises. It’s like riding in a car that has a jerky transmission. Edit: I've become aware that this is a rewrite.
  • The actions and developments of the first exploration log, usually a solid strong point for the author, seem kinda rote and delivered in an almost uninspired way. (The slack is made up for in the second exploration log imo, and is more like it with respect to one of the author’s strengths.) The team goes through a series of familiar motions that repeats familiar information and feels like there’s no finesse to the flow of it. Something that might have to do with it is that I don’t believe that these are actual MTF members; minus the dialogue with Command, they might as well be the young adults they’re looking for, or D-Class personnel for that matter.
  • “ATL-9 Sherman: Over here, you cocksucker!” Why is the inclusion of this okay?
  • "The camera pans across to see dozens of other figures at first, and then potentially hundreds, all hanging upside down above the power lines.”
I was confused by this line initially. I had to go back and read some of the exploration logs because I seemed to be misremembering what the article said the orientation of the victims were in prior to this. I’m going to block quote what I went back and read:

As they come over a large hill, the power line stanchions become visible. Strung across the high tension lines are numerous figures, bound in ropes, hanging upside down by nooses from the lines.

This same phrase, "hung upside down”, is used in the same way by the first MTF when they see the missing kids. It’s used in the same way in the discovery addendum, there in direct quotations. I realized that the article isn’t cherry-picking orientations; it gets an effect from being ambiguous with the phrasing. In fact, I think it is the fulcrum of the whole article. “Hanging upside down” could mean, as we and as in-universe people hearing the folk tales would first assume from the language, that the people are hanging (properly) by their ankles,1 with their heads pointing toward the ground; it could also mean what I think the article is saying all along that the individual is actually defying gravity by an as-straightforward-as-you-can-mean noose. I believe that this was intentional,2 and so in the way I experienced the article, is really clever. That kind of unexpected play is the stuff good scary stories are made of.

So what is this bullet point doing in the Criticism section? The lack of clarity used to keep the wool over my eyes breaks immersion for me completely. I want it to be, because this is a good scary story, but it isn't believable to me in-universe and I can’t get over it. Neither the initial MTF or the ATL-9 members remarked — to themselves or to Command — that Jack was found, not just hanging upside down, but let-me-make-this-unequivocally-clear hanging against gravity. It’s odd in retrospect. I felt like it was a little too forced of a play. So for me, the break in believability is stronger than the good effects gained in the story-telling. (I would feel good if others reading this decide this is impressive enough of a writing move to upvote the article, because anywhere where believability and suspension of disbelief isn't a primary concern and part of the challenge, this would be a masterstroke.)
Verdict
– hide block

This started at a snail’s slow pace for me, but really picked up by the end. I think the author succeeded in the campfire story vibe very well and at the same time somehow incorporated some cosmic/surreal elements into it extremely well too. Unfortunately, it just barely didn’t climb above the threshold for me. This has more to do with the site’s idiosyncrasies as a medium, and its challenge regarding the suspension of disbelief, rather than anything subpar in this story. I know that is only partially fair to the author; a failure of the art form of the SCP article to accommodate a great story, is more like it. Really really good piece of literature here.

Edit: (After reading the comments.) For what words are worth that an upvote isn’t, bonus props to this being a rewrite too. I’ll say that I think the author started with some difficult ingredients to work with — it is a thing that kills you with a recovered-footage, no-survivor, teen-slasher-murder-movie cast. What is made from that is really strong and is a testament to the author’s skill and ingenuity. Like a job well-done on an episode of Chopped.

Footnotes
1. By “nooses” though? Eh, it made more sense to interpret “noose” loosely if need be above assuming people are gravitationally upside down with no clearer context. Plus, technically people could be hung by ankles using a noose.
2. The author is too aware for it to be an oops. The reveal is even teased earlier, played with via the MTF member Teter in the first exploration log who almost says it: “they’re hung upside down above (heavy breathing)…it’s right behind us” (above… the power lines?!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would be something to drop a (polite!) PM to the author about, in case they don't see the forum post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noticed a possible mistake.

The caption in the header says:

Access road to SCP-3785. Image recovered from cell phone footage. See Addendum 3785.3 (3785.AV.01) for details.

But the recovered cell phone data is the 4th addendum and the 3rd addendum is the second exploration log. If this was intentional, please let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little confused on some directions, like how people were hanging upside down but also floating above the lines at the same time? Other than that, an exciting read. It's a really interesting concept and sounds like realistic modern folklore. Exploration logs played out like a horror movie in my head. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was very pleasantly sinister, and had a good frantic energy which kept up throughout. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My serious opinion would be the same as above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is cleverly constructed, with the cosmic horror providing a well-judged shift of gears at almost exactly the point where this could have otherwise been a little familiar. I also really enjoyed the fragmented nature of the final addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The imagery is among the most evocative I’ve ever seen on the site. Combined with the deeply unsettling concept that leaves a lot up to speculation, this is an easy +1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much this. It's an eyesore for me, and I'm not a fan of seeing everybody try to adapt it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OMG selfies #nofilter #murder #sacrificetotheoldgods

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have an uncanny ability to steal spots that I've wanted, kaktus. Aside from that, the skip isn't too shabby, if not a bit formulaic. I don't mind; it's good stuff, and I'm freaked out.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
plot twist: the headlights are actually a pair of SCP-745

In all seriousness, the addition of cosmic horror really did add an amazing surreality to the article. Solid +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading this a couple months ago and being creeped out by it. I think the cosmic horror bits detract from some of the classic, simple horror feel that I felt the original had, but it still works.

Solid +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohh, what the butt? Okay, well, have a re-upvote then. c.c

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a rewrite of what was formerly SCP-947, before it was self-deleted approximately seven months ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, I don't get it. Is this a rework? Did I see a sandbox post the first time? Because I clearly remember this, and I'm not sure why I'm seeing it again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't remember if I upvoted this last time, but I think I did?

I think the cosmic horror stuff puts an interesting twist on this, and leaves me scratching my head about -1's motives in a neat way.

On the downside, the log around the cosmic stuff goes hard into purple prose territory. It was enough to take me out of it.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nobody said that! I just think special formats should be used in cases where it makes sense to break said format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idk I was just doing it because I liked how it looked. I guess I won't do it anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much how I feel. I'd call it a format screw but it keeps popping up so often that I feel like I need to alter aesthetic in order to stand out, which shouldn't be the case. I liked the hype for the b i g stuff

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The story itself is good, and I like it better than last time. But I have to say, I'm really getting sick of seeing the banner in every article. It used to be you only used it when something was a big deal in universe, like 3000 or your second 001. It hyped me up, and prepared me for an anomaly with earthshattering implications. Now its just filler padding that doesn't tell me anything and just means I have to scroll down more to reach the actual article. The effect has been more than ruined; its been actively reversed. I wish you'd start saving it for when shit gets real again.

Still a +1 for the story though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. nice selfie

2. this reminds me of Deathproof

3. It's good. I think it has a series III vibe to it

4. i'm still salty about the use of these fancy formats. That means I have to do more work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this the first time around, and I still like it now. The cosmic horror bits added some flair to the pretty standard, but well executed, slasher villain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rednecks work hard. Evil works hard. Therefor, rednecks are Evil. +1

item.title: Jasper Pt. 2: Redneck Boogaloo
item.content:encoded:
Those of you playing the at-home version of the SCP Wiki might remember this article from a while ago. It's something I really enjoyed putting together so I thought I'd give it another look at. Thanks to everyone who gave this a look over in chat, and my brother for whom this article stars and is dedicated to.

I hope you all like it as much as I enjoyed writing it!

Also p.s. the images are all either my own, or my brother's, and released to me because I'm bigger than he is.
